narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put together an impressive profile that includes an undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and a standout LSAT score of 40—well above the typical student's 36.7 average. Your Year 1 school decile ranking of 1st place is exceptional, though your Year 3 decile of 2nd is still strong but a bit lower than average. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 40.9% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try—high enough to trigger a rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with the lowest predicted risk of failure. This decision hinged most heavily on a few key factors from your record. Your outstanding LSAT score was the strongest positive force, strongly pushing the prediction toward bar exam success and showing how it sets you apart from most applicants. On the flip side, your Year 3 decile ranking had the biggest negative pull, nudging the model toward predicting failure because it was below the typical student's performance in that area. Your Year 1 decile, while elite, also contributed somewhat against you relative to expectations, adding a modest push toward the failure prediction. Your undergraduate GPA offered some helpful counterbalance, leaning toward success, though not as powerfully as your LSAT. Meanwhile, your upper-middle family income played a small role in tipping toward higher risk, and your full-time enrollment status provided a minor boost in the right direction. Compared to typical students, your LSAT shines brightly, but your later-year deciles lag behind averages, which amplified their influence in this close call. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT if possible to solidify that strength, boosting your Year 3 performance through targeted study or extracurriculars, and reapplying to schools with more holistic reviews—many succeed on second tries by addressing these exact gaps. You've got real potential here; this is just one model's snapshot, and with persistence, you can turn it around. Keep pushing.
